title: Graph Coloring (4 nodes, 3 colors)
summary: Assign colors to a graph's nodes so no two adjacent nodes share a color.
problem_statement: I have a 4-node graph with edges (0,1), (1,2), (2,0), (0,3). The triangle {0,1,2} forces all three of its nodes to take different colors. Find a valid 3-coloring using colors A, B, C. Each node gets exactly one color, and no edge has both endpoints in the same color.
real_world_example: Assigning frequencies to radio towers so neighboring towers don't interfere, scheduling exams so a student never has two conflicting exams in the same slot, or assigning compiler registers so live variables don't share one.

expected_pattern.variables: x_{n,c} in {0, 1} — whether node n is colored c (12 binaries for 4 nodes × 3 colors)
expected_pattern.objective: minimize 0 — pure feasibility, every valid coloring is optimal
expected_pattern.constraints: for each node n: sum_c x_{n,c} = 1; for each edge (n,m) and color c: x_{n,c} + x_{m,c} <= 1

expected_solution_summary: One valid coloring: node 0 = color A, node 1 = color B, node 2 = color C, node 3 = color B. (Any permutation of the colors is also a valid answer.)
learning_notes: Graph coloring is the first place students hit *one-hot* encoding: a discrete variable (color choice) becomes a *family* of binaries with an equality constraint summing to 1. The same trick appears in vehicle routing, machine assignment, time slot allocation. The number of binary variables grows as nodes × colors, so the encoding ceiling matters.

learning_objectives: Encode a discrete-choice variable as a one-hot family of binaries
Spot the 'sum to 1' equality constraint as the one-hot trademark
Estimate the variable count for nodes-by-colors / jobs-by-slots problems
